- '[[openalex-2605.26770-quality-without-usefulness-llm-generated-xai-narratives-as-t]]'
title: Closing the XAI Quality-Usefulness Gap
---

**Background:** Natural language explanations (NLEs) generated by LLMs from post-hoc XAI attributions consistently score highly on quality metrics, but their impact on downstream task performance remains empirically contentious. Empirical evidence suggests a "Quality-Usefulness Gap" where high-quality explanations do not enhance task-based decision-making and, in certain out-of-distribution scenarios, can degrade performance through false reassurance.

**Question / Future Work:** It remains unclear how to systematically design NLE systems that move beyond merely optimizing for perceived text quality to optimizing for measurable downstream usefulness. Specifically, there is an open need to develop and validate intervention strategies—such as cognitive forcing functions, dissenting explanations, or uncertainty-aware formats—that successfully engage users in central processing rather than the peripheral, confidence-inflating heuristics currently observed. Future work must resolve whether such interventions are effective across varying domains and whether human users exhibit the same quality-usefulness gap observed with LLM-based judges.

**Why It Matters:** This question is central to the field of XAI because it challenges the reliance on surface-level quality metrics (coherence, fluency, plausibility) and highlights the danger of LLM-generated explanations acting as persuasive trust heuristics that mask model failures.

**Evidence:** Whether any of these closes the gap in our LLM-judge setting – let alone with human users – is an open empirical question worth answering.
